scrum.title: Scrum Sprint Simulator
scrum.tagline: You're the Scrum Master. Survive 12 decisions across one sprint.
scrum.eli5: Scrum is a framework where teams work in fixed-length cycles called sprints. Each sprint, you make dozens of small judgment calls. Get them wrong and your team grinds; get them right and you ship working software every two weeks. This game throws 12 of the most common decisions at you, in the order they happen in a sprint.

rounds.prompt: DAY 0 — Sprint Planning. The Product Owner brings a list of 18 items totaling 70 story points. Your team's average velocity is 35. What do you do?
choices: Commit to all 18 to keep the PO happy
Help the team negotiate down to a forecast that matches velocity (~35 points), with a clear Sprint Goal
Skip planning and let the team pull work as they go
Halve the sprint length to fit more sprints
rounds.answer: Help the team negotiate down to a forecast that matches velocity (~35 points), with a clear Sprint Goal
rounds.hint: Sprint forecasts come from the DEV team, anchored on velocity, organized around a Sprint Goal.

rounds.prompt: DAY 1 — A senior dev says 'daily standups are a waste, I'd rather just send a Slack update.' What do you do?
choices: Cancel the standup — Slack is fine
Explain the standup is for the TEAM (planning the next 24h, surfacing blockers), not status reporting, and invite them to shape the format
rounds.answer: Explain the standup is for the TEAM (planning the next 24h, surfacing blockers), not status reporting, and invite them to shape the format
rounds.hint: Daily Scrum is a team event for the team — not a status meeting for management.

rounds.prompt: DAY 2 — During the standup, a dev says 'I'm blocked by Legal — they need 2 days to review the privacy text.' What's your move as SM?
choices: Wait 2 days and re-plan if needed
Take the blocker offline — work to expedite Legal RIGHT AFTER the standup; protect the rest of the meeting's time-box
Send the legal email yourself during the standup
Reassign the work to another team member
rounds.answer: Take the blocker offline — work to expedite Legal RIGHT AFTER the standup; protect the rest of the meeting's time-box
rounds.hint: Standup surfaces blockers; SM removes them outside the standup.

rounds.prompt: DAY 4 — The PO asks to add a 5-point urgent feature mid-sprint. The team committed to 35 points and is on track. What's the right response?
choices: Add it — agile teams accept change at any time
Reject it outright — the sprint is locked
Defer to the next sprint, OR if truly urgent, surface the trade-off (drop equivalent work)
rounds.answer: Defer to the next sprint, OR if truly urgent, surface the trade-off (drop equivalent work)
rounds.hint: The sprint goal is stable. Trade-offs must be explicit.

rounds.prompt: DAY 5 — A dev finishes a story but it doesn't meet the team's Definition of Done (no tests written). What do you do?
choices: Move it to Done anyway — close enough
Block 'Done' — work isn't complete until DoD is met; coach the team to add tests
Lower the DoD to match what shipped
Bypass the PO and accept it yourself
rounds.answer: Block 'Done' — work isn't complete until DoD is met; coach the team to add tests
rounds.hint: Definition of Done is non-negotiable. It's why you have it.

rounds.prompt: DAY 6 — A stakeholder pings the team directly with new requirements during the day. What do you do as SM?
choices: Tell the team to ignore stakeholders
Protect the team's focus — route the request through the PO so it can be prioritized in the backlog
Add the request to the current sprint
Hide the stakeholder's emails from the team
rounds.answer: Protect the team's focus — route the request through the PO so it can be prioritized in the backlog
rounds.hint: PO owns the backlog. SM protects the team from interruption.

rounds.prompt: DAY 7 — Velocity is dropping. Last 3 sprints: 38, 32, 28. What do you do?
choices: Pressure the team to commit more next sprint
Bring it to the retrospective — investigate root cause with the team (burnout? tech debt? scope creep?)
Hide the trend from the PO
rounds.answer: Bring it to the retrospective — investigate root cause with the team (burnout? tech debt? scope creep?)
rounds.hint: Retro is where the team owns process improvement.

rounds.prompt: DAY 8 — A dev says 'this isn't agile because we still write requirements.' How do you respond?
choices: Agile values working software OVER comprehensive docs — but it doesn't say 'no docs'. Write enough to deliver value; no more.
rounds.answer: Agile values working software OVER comprehensive docs — but it doesn't say 'no docs'. Write enough to deliver value; no more.
rounds.hint: 'Over' doesn't mean 'instead of'. Both sides matter; left is valued more.

rounds.prompt: DAY 9 — The PO is on vacation. A stakeholder needs a backlog decision today. What do you do?
choices: Make the decision yourself as SM
Defer to the PO's designated proxy (if one exists), or document the request for the PO's return — don't pretend to own PO decisions
rounds.answer: Defer to the PO's designated proxy (if one exists), or document the request for the PO's return — don't pretend to own PO decisions
rounds.hint: SM ≠ PO. Role boundaries matter even under pressure.

rounds.prompt: DAY 10 — Sprint Review (demo). A stakeholder says 'we'd actually prefer feature X to look completely different.' What happens?
choices: Welcome the feedback; the PO captures it and re-orders the backlog for next sprint
Promise to redo it this sprint
Skip the next review to avoid conflict
rounds.answer: Welcome the feedback; the PO captures it and re-orders the backlog for next sprint
rounds.hint: Review is where you INSPECT the product and ADAPT the backlog.

rounds.prompt: DAY 10 — Sprint Retrospective. The same complaint comes up for the 3rd sprint in a row: 'CI pipeline is too slow.' What's the right action?
choices: Commit to a concrete action this sprint with an owner and date — track it on the team board
Tell the team to stop complaining
rounds.answer: Commit to a concrete action this sprint with an owner and date — track it on the team board
rounds.hint: Retros fail when actions accumulate without follow-through.

rounds.prompt: POST-SPRINT — A new exec joins and says 'I want to be the Scrum Master AND assign tasks directly to devs.' How do you respond?
choices: Explain that the SM is a servant leader, NOT a task assigner — the dev team self-organizes around the Sprint Goal
Agree and become a passive observer
rounds.answer: Explain that the SM is a servant leader, NOT a task assigner — the dev team self-organizes around the Sprint Goal
rounds.hint: Self-organization is a core Scrum value.

scrumAdvanced.title: Scaling Scrum & Backlog Mastery
scrumAdvanced.tagline: 12 advanced calls — scaling frameworks, refinement, story splitting.
scrumAdvanced.eli5: Once you've mastered single-team Scrum, the real-world questions get harder. Multiple teams on one product? Stories too big for a sprint? A 200-person 'agile transformation'? This game tests the next level: backlog refinement, splitting techniques, and what each scaling framework actually does.

rounds.prompt: BACKLOG REFINEMENT — How much team time should backlog refinement take per sprint?
choices: 10% or less of the team's capacity, spread across the sprint
rounds.answer: 10% or less of the team's capacity, spread across the sprint
rounds.hint: Refinement is an ongoing activity — small, frequent, not a giant ceremony.

rounds.prompt: BACKLOG REFINEMENT — Who is responsible for refining the product backlog?
choices: The Product Owner with collaboration from the Dev Team
rounds.answer: The Product Owner with collaboration from the Dev Team
rounds.hint: PO owns the backlog; dev team helps split, size, and clarify.

rounds.prompt: STORY SPLITTING — A story is 21 points but the team's sprint capacity is 35. The team has never delivered anything over 13 points before. What should happen?
choices: Pull it as-is; sprint capacity allows it
Split the story into smaller, independently valuable pieces before bringing it into the sprint
Move it to the next sprint untouched
Defer the decision to the Scrum Master
rounds.answer: Split the story into smaller, independently valuable pieces before bringing it into the sprint
rounds.hint: INVEST → S = Small. If it's bigger than the team's typical chunk, split it.

rounds.prompt: STORY SPLITTING — Which is a GOOD way to split a large story?
choices: Split by workflow steps (search → filter → purchase) so each slice is independently valuable
rounds.answer: Split by workflow steps (search → filter → purchase) so each slice is independently valuable
rounds.hint: Vertical slices > horizontal layers. Each piece should deliver value alone.

rounds.prompt: STORY SPLITTING — A 'spike' is…
choices: A time-boxed research/prototype activity to reduce uncertainty before committing to a story
rounds.answer: A time-boxed research/prototype activity to reduce uncertainty before committing to a story
rounds.hint: When you can't estimate, do a small spike, then re-estimate.

rounds.prompt: SCALING — LeSS (Large-Scale Scrum) is BEST described as…
choices: Multiple Scrum Masters reporting to one chief SM
One Product Owner, one Product Backlog, multiple teams pulling from it — Scrum 'scaled up' with minimal added process
A 6-month planning event for hundreds of people
rounds.answer: One Product Owner, one Product Backlog, multiple teams pulling from it — Scrum 'scaled up' with minimal added process
rounds.hint: LeSS philosophy: scale by SUBTRACTING process, not adding it.

rounds.prompt: SCALING — SAFe's 'Program Increment (PI)' is…
choices: A time-box of typically 8–12 weeks containing multiple sprints, planned at PI Planning
A specific story in the backlog
rounds.answer: A time-box of typically 8–12 weeks containing multiple sprints, planned at PI Planning
rounds.hint: PI = the rhythm SAFe uses to align multiple teams (the Agile Release Train).

rounds.prompt: SCALING — In SAFe, an 'Agile Release Train (ART)' is…
choices: A single Scrum team of 9 people
A long-lived team-of-teams (typically 5–12 teams, 50–125 people) that delivers value together
rounds.answer: A long-lived team-of-teams (typically 5–12 teams, 50–125 people) that delivers value together
rounds.hint: ART = the unit of value delivery at the Program level in SAFe.

rounds.prompt: SCALING — 'Scrum of Scrums' is…
choices: Multiple teams' Scrum Masters meeting to coordinate cross-team dependencies
A super-sprint where all teams plan together
A way to fire the Scrum Master
rounds.answer: Multiple teams' Scrum Masters meeting to coordinate cross-team dependencies
rounds.hint: Same idea as Daily Scrum, one level up — dependencies between teams, not within.

rounds.prompt: SCALING — Your company adopts SAFe and a stakeholder says 'now the Product Owner reports to the Release Train Engineer.' Is that right?
choices: Yes — RTE outranks PO in SAFe
No — PO and RTE are PEERS with different focus. PO owns the team's backlog; RTE facilitates the ART
rounds.answer: No — PO and RTE are PEERS with different focus. PO owns the team's backlog; RTE facilitates the ART
rounds.hint: Different responsibilities, equal seats.

rounds.prompt: BACKLOG MASTERY — Which is a strong sign that a backlog is poorly refined?
choices: Items at the top are crisp; items deep in the backlog are vague — that's correct
Top items don't pass team's Definition of Ready (no AC, sizing unclear, dependencies unknown)
The backlog has more than 30 items
rounds.answer: Top items don't pass team's Definition of Ready (no AC, sizing unclear, dependencies unknown)
rounds.hint: Top items must be READY for the next sprint. Vague tops = refinement gap.

rounds.prompt: DEPENDENCIES — Two teams discover mid-sprint they both need the same API to ship. What's the BEST first move?
choices: Each team builds their own API
Surface the dependency at Scrum of Scrums; coordinate ownership + delivery sequence; document the agreement
Both teams stop until management decides
Wait until the next PI Planning
rounds.answer: Surface the dependency at Scrum of Scrums; coordinate ownership + delivery sequence; document the agreement
rounds.hint: Cross-team dependencies = SoS material. Surface fast, resolve fast.

xp.title: XP Practices Picker
xp.tagline: 12 coding situations. Pick the eXtreme Programming practice that helps.
xp.eli5: Extreme Programming (XP) is an agile method that focuses on engineering EXCELLENCE. Where Scrum manages the work, XP says: 'do specific technical practices to build quality in.' This game shows you 12 real situations and asks: which XP practice solves this?

rounds.prompt: Two devs solve the same bug differently. The codebase has 4 different ways to do logging. What XP practice would prevent this?
rounds.answer: Coding Standards
rounds.hint: When the team agrees on standards, divergent solutions disappear.

rounds.prompt: A junior dev keeps shipping bugs because they don't catch edge cases. What XP practice catches these EARLIEST?
rounds.answer: Test-Driven Development (TDD)
rounds.hint: TDD makes you write the test BEFORE the code — edge cases get named up front.

rounds.prompt: A senior dev disappears for 2 days and the team can't make progress because they're the only one who knows the auth module. What XP practice prevents this?
rounds.answer: Collective Code Ownership
rounds.hint: Collective ownership = anyone can change any code; no 'mine' modules.

rounds.prompt: Devs merge code once a week. By Friday, conflicts are a nightmare. What XP practice fixes this?
choices: Continuous Integration (merge to main daily, run automated tests)
rounds.answer: Continuous Integration (merge to main daily, run automated tests)
rounds.hint: CI = integrate small, integrate often, integrate continuously.

rounds.prompt: A dev writes a 300-line function 'because the design got out of hand.' The team grumbles but ships it. What XP practice should they apply NEXT?
rounds.answer: Refactoring
rounds.hint: Refactoring = improving structure WITHOUT changing behavior.

rounds.prompt: The team works 60-hour weeks for 3 sprints. Velocity is now dropping. What XP value is being violated?
rounds.answer: Sustainable Pace
rounds.hint: XP says burnout kills throughput. Pace = a marathon, not a sprint.

rounds.prompt: A dev wants to over-architect a system 'in case we need 10 features later.' What XP practice pushes back?
choices: Simple Design (do the simplest thing that could possibly work)
rounds.answer: Simple Design (do the simplest thing that could possibly work)
rounds.hint: YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It. Build for now, refactor when 'later' arrives.

rounds.prompt: The team and the customer have wildly different mental models of the product. Devs build the wrong thing. What XP practice closes the gap?
choices: Metaphor (a shared story that explains the system)
rounds.answer: Metaphor (a shared story that explains the system)
rounds.hint: Metaphor = a shared mental model so devs and customer mean the same thing.

rounds.prompt: The PO is in another office and devs only see them once a month. Requirements are constantly wrong. What XP practice fixes this?
choices: On-site Customer (PO works alongside the team)
rounds.answer: On-site Customer (PO works alongside the team)
rounds.hint: Tight feedback loop — customer questions answered in minutes, not weeks.

rounds.prompt: The team ships a giant release every 6 months. By the time it lands, half the features are out of date. What XP practice fixes this?
rounds.answer: Small Releases (ship working software frequently)
rounds.hint: Small releases = early value + faster learning.

rounds.prompt: Two devs constantly disagree on solutions and produce conflicting code. What XP practice helps them align in real-time?
rounds.answer: Pair Programming
rounds.hint: Two people, one screen, real-time agreement and review.

rounds.prompt: The team has built features no one uses. What XP practice surfaces this earliest?
choices: Planning Game + Small Releases (so you learn what users actually want)
rounds.answer: Planning Game + Small Releases (so you learn what users actually want)
rounds.hint: Planning Game = customer prioritizes; Small Releases = early feedback on whether prioritization was right.

predictive.title: Waterfall Phase-Gate Walkthrough
predictive.tagline: Pilot a predictive project through 12 critical decisions.
predictive.eli5: A predictive (waterfall) project moves through formal phases with GATES between them. At each gate, you decide whether to proceed, kill, or rework. This game walks you through one project, end-to-end, with the decisions an experienced PM has to make.

rounds.prompt: INITIATING — A sponsor asks you to 'just start' the project before the charter is signed. What do you do?
choices: Decline politely — the charter is what AUTHORIZES the project and gives you authority over resources
rounds.answer: Decline politely — the charter is what AUTHORIZES the project and gives you authority over resources
rounds.hint: No charter = no authority = no project.

rounds.prompt: INITIATING — You identify 14 stakeholders. The sponsor says 'only the CFO matters, skip the rest.' What do you do?
choices: Document them all in the stakeholder register, classify by power × interest, and engage appropriately — missing stakeholders surface late as blockers
Document only the most senior ones
Wait for issues to identify themselves
rounds.answer: Document them all in the stakeholder register, classify by power × interest, and engage appropriately — missing stakeholders surface late as blockers
rounds.hint: Surprise stakeholders late = drama. Capture all early.

rounds.prompt: PLANNING — You're building the WBS. A team member says 'let's just put activities in the WBS.' What do you tell them?
choices: WBS shows DELIVERABLES (nouns), the schedule shows ACTIVITIES (verbs) — keep them separate
rounds.answer: WBS shows DELIVERABLES (nouns), the schedule shows ACTIVITIES (verbs) — keep them separate
rounds.hint: WBS = decomposition of SCOPE, not the schedule.

rounds.prompt: PLANNING — The critical path is 90 days, but the customer needs delivery in 75 days. You can either CRASH or FAST TRACK. The project is safety-critical. Which is safer?
choices: Crashing (add resources — costs more, but doesn't introduce rework risk)
Fast tracking (parallelize — cheaper, but risks rework)
Cut scope without telling the customer
rounds.answer: Crashing (add resources — costs more, but doesn't introduce rework risk)
rounds.hint: Fast tracking trades schedule for risk. Safety-critical = avoid added risk.

rounds.prompt: PLANNING — Three estimates for activity A: O=4, M=6, P=14. Customer asks for the PERT estimate.
rounds.answer: 7 days

rounds.prompt: PLANNING — A vendor offers two contract types: FIXED PRICE or COST REIMBURSABLE. The scope is still fuzzy. Which contract is right?
choices: Cost Reimbursable — the buyer absorbs cost variability while scope clarifies
rounds.answer: Cost Reimbursable — the buyer absorbs cost variability while scope clarifies
rounds.hint: FP assumes clear scope. Fuzzy scope = vendor would price for worst case anyway.

rounds.prompt: EXECUTING — A change request arrives. Stakeholder says 'it's small, just do it.' What do you do?
choices: Route through Integrated Change Control: assess impact → CCB approval → update baselines → communicate
Add to scope only if free
rounds.answer: Route through Integrated Change Control: assess impact → CCB approval → update baselines → communicate
rounds.hint: Every change goes through ICC, period. Skipping it = baseline drift.

rounds.prompt: EXECUTING — EVM check at month 6: PV = $300K, EV = $240K, AC = $290K. What does this tell you?
choices: Behind schedule (SPI = 0.8) AND over budget (CPI = 0.83). Triage both.
rounds.answer: Behind schedule (SPI = 0.8) AND over budget (CPI = 0.83). Triage both.

rounds.prompt: EXECUTING — A risk you identified in planning just became an issue. The vendor confirmed late delivery. What do you do?
choices: Move from risk register to issue log; execute the planned RESPONSE; communicate impact to stakeholders
rounds.answer: Move from risk register to issue log; execute the planned RESPONSE; communicate impact to stakeholders
rounds.hint: Risk becomes issue = trigger the planned response.

rounds.prompt: EXECUTING — The team is at 95% complete. Sponsor asks to add 'one more small feature.' What do you do?
choices: Run it through change control like any other change — assess scope, schedule, cost impact
Add it but don't tell anyone
rounds.answer: Run it through change control like any other change — assess scope, schedule, cost impact
rounds.hint: 'Small' changes near the end are often anything but small.

rounds.prompt: CLOSING — Deliverables are accepted but the customer lists 3 minor defects to fix in 30 days. What's your move?
choices: Document the CONDITIONAL acceptance, track defects as punch-list/warranty, plan formal closure once items are resolved
Refuse to close until all defects are fixed
Transfer everything to ops and walk away
rounds.answer: Document the CONDITIONAL acceptance, track defects as punch-list/warranty, plan formal closure once items are resolved
rounds.hint: Conditional acceptance ≠ final closure.

rounds.prompt: CLOSING — Last activity: do a lessons-learned session. Stakeholder asks 'is this really needed?' What do you say?
choices: Yes — capture wins, misses, and recommendations in the lessons-learned register so future projects don't repeat the same mistakes
rounds.answer: Yes — capture wins, misses, and recommendations in the lessons-learned register so future projects don't repeat the same mistakes
rounds.hint: Lessons learned is how the org gets better. Without it, every project starts from scratch.
